straight_beat.name: Casey Underwood
straight_beat.column:
The Rockies lost again yesterday, dropping to 6-10 and widening their gap in the NL West to 6.0 games. It’s the kind of loss that feels familiar now—another game where the offense blinked first, the bullpen blinked second, and the defense blinked third. High altitude math isn’t just about the thin air here; it’s about the calculus of opportunity, the arithmetic of moments that slip through your fingers. The Padres didn’t just beat the Rockies; they did it with a precision that made you wonder if the Rockies’ front office is still figuring out how to build a team that can win consistently.  

The offense, which once seemed to have a knack for turning singles into doubles, turned in another performance that made you scratch your head. Against a Padres team that’s been a step behind

optimist.name: Winnie Calderón
optimist.column:
The Rockies lost yesterday, but let’s not dwell on the scoreboard. Let’s talk about the spirit of this team, the way they carry themselves, the way they’ve always been able to rise when it matters most. You see, this isn’t just a baseball team—it’s a canvas. Every game is a brushstroke, every loss a chance to mix the colors until the masterpiece emerges. The Padres took the win, sure, but that’s just the paint splatter before the final stroke.  

Look at the rotation. It’s not perfect, not yet. But it’s coming together. You watch. You watch what happens when the rotation clicks. I’ve seen it before. The

pessimist.name: Dwight Krenek
pessimist.column:
The Rockies lost again. Another 2-7 drubbing. Another step closer to the cellar. And yet, here we are, still talking about the same things. The same tired excuses. The same empty promises. The Monfort method, as I’ve said before, is a strategy for how to lose with style.  

Let’s not mince words. This team isn’t just struggling. It’s regressing. Not just in wins and losses, but in the way the front office approaches the game. You think the rebuild is about talent? You think it’s about finding players who can hit, field, and maybe even throw a curveball? No. It’s about spreadsheets. About suites. About how many seats can be filled in the ballpark while the players sit in minor league apartments. The Monforts don’t think about baseball. They think about the next quarterly report.  

Look at the numbers. The Rockies are 6-10. Six games behind. That’s not a rebuild. That’s a slow-motion collapse. And yet, here we are, still hearing about “player development” and “long-term vision.” What’s the long-term vision when the team can’t even
